abstract: In recent years, scholarship has demonstrated that the American public engages with foreign policy through relatively stable orientations toward international affairs, shaped by a blend of demographic, ideological, and partisan factors. Despite growing speculation regarding the role of religion in defining these worldviews, rigorous empirical research on the subject remains scarce. This article addresses this gap by utilizing the 2012 Chicago Council on Global Affairs survey to map American religious groups onto the classic dimensions of foreign policy attitudes identified by Wittkopf (1990): militant internationalism and cooperative internationalism. The findings reveal distinct religious constituencies for each perspective. Evangelical Protestants and religious traditionalists across various faiths emerge as the primary supporters of militant internationalism, whereas ethnoreligious minorities and religious modernists are significantly more likely to favor cooperative internationalism.
